# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains method and benchmark evidence about an open-sourced coding agent stack, including claimed architectural components, evaluation setup, benchmark scores, ablation results, and repeated-run memory effects.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: claim-support
citation: Wang et al., Confucius Code Agent, abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper presents CCA as an open-sourced AI software engineer intended to operate at industrial scale.

## Source Excerpt

“We present the Confucius Code Agent (CCA), an open-sourced AI software engineer that can operate at an industrial scale.”

## Note

This is an author claim about system identity and intended operating scope.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Confucius Code Agent, abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The Confucius SDK is described as combining a unified orchestrator, hierarchical working memory, persistent note-taking across sessions, and a modular extension module for tool use.

## Source Excerpt

“The SDK introduces a unified orchestrator with hierarchical working memory for long-context reasoning, a persistent note-taking system for cross-session continual learning, and a modular extension module for robust tool use.”

## Note

This evidence states the named components the authors say make up the SDK.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 1, AX/UX/DX
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The SDK is framed around three design perspectives: Agent Experience, User Experience, and Developer Experience.

## Source Excerpt

“CCA is built atop the Confucius SDK, an open-sourced agent development platform designed around three complementary perspectives: Agent Experience (AX), User Experience (UX), and Developer Experience (DX).”

## Note

This is a design-structure statement about how the platform is organized.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.1, context management
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The SDK uses hierarchical working memory with adaptive context compression for large-repository agent runs.

## Source Excerpt

“The Confucius SDK addresses this by providing an explicit agent context management layer that combines hierarchical working memory with adaptive context compression.”

## Note

This evidence describes the specific context-management mechanism.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.2, note-taking agent
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The note-taking agent stores persistent notes as Markdown files in a file-system-like tree.

## Source Excerpt

“Persistent notes are stored as Markdown files in a file-system-like tree: each session has an associated directory, under which the note-taking agent can create paths such as project/architecture.md, research/findings.md, or solutions/bug_fix.md.”

## Note

This is an operational detail about the note storage format and organization.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.2, hindsight notes
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The note-taking layer is described as recording failure cases alongside successful solutions.

## Source Excerpt

“A distinctive aspect of the Confucius SDK’s note-taking is its emphasis on hindsight notes for failures. The note-taking layer encourages agents to record not only successful solutions but also compilation errors, runtime exceptions, and unproductive strategies, together with eventual resolutions or reasons for abandonment.”

## Note

This evidence ties long-term memory to explicit preservation of failures, not just successes.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.3, extensions
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The SDK factors most agent behaviors into extensions that register typed callbacks on the orchestrator loop.

## Source Excerpt

“Concretely, an extension in the Confucius SDK is a typed configuration object that registers callbacks (e.g., on_input_messages, on_plain_text, on_tag, on_llm_output). At each step of the orchestrator’s while-loop, these callbacks are invoked in a fixed order.”

## Note

This is a direct implementation detail for how capabilities are attached to the core loop.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.4, meta-agent
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The Meta-agent is described as building and refining agents through an explicit build-test-improve loop.

## Source Excerpt

“Confucius SDK addresses this by introducing a Meta Agent, an agent that automatically builds and refines other agents through an explicit build-test-improve loop.”

## Note

This evidence states the claimed role of the meta-agent in agent development.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 3.1, SWE-Bench-Pro setup
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The main SWE-Bench-Pro evaluation uses the public split with 731 tasks and reports mean Resolve@1 across three runs.

## Source Excerpt

“For main results, we evaluate CCA on the SWE-Bench-Pro (Deng et al., 2025) public split consisting of 731 tasks... We report mean Resolve@1 across three runs.”

## Note

This is an evaluation-setup detail, not a performance result by itself.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Table 1, Claude 4 Sonnet
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On SWE-Bench-Pro with Claude 4 Sonnet, CCA is reported at 45.5% Resolve Rate versus 42.7% for SWE-Agent.

## Source Excerpt

“Claude 4 Sonnet SWE-Agent (Yang et al., 2024) 42.7” and “CCA 45.5”.

## Note

This is a table-reported benchmark comparison under the same listed backbone model.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Table 1, Claude 4.5 Sonnet
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On SWE-Bench-Pro with Claude 4.5 Sonnet, CCA is reported at 52.7% Resolve Rate versus 45.8% for Live-SWE-Agent.

## Source Excerpt

“Claude 4.5 Sonnet ... Live-SWE-Agent (Xia et al., 2025) 45.8” and “CCA 52.7”.

## Note

This evidence is limited to the benchmark comparison reported in Table 1.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Table 1, Claude 4.5 Opus
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On SWE-Bench-Pro with Claude 4.5 Opus, CCA is reported at 54.3% Resolve Rate versus 52.0% for Anthropic’s proprietary scaffold.

## Source Excerpt

“Claude 4.5 Opus Proprietary to Anthropic* 52.0” and “CCA 54.3”.

## Note

This evidence is a reported benchmark comparison against a proprietary scaffold entry in Table 1.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Section 3.4.1, context ablation
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On a 100-example SWE-Bench-Pro subset with Claude 4 Sonnet, advanced context management is reported to improve Resolve@1 from 42.0 to 48.6.

## Source Excerpt

“For Claude 4 Sonnet, advanced context management improves Resolve@1 from 42.0 to 48.6 on this subset (a +6.6 performance gain).”

## Note

This is a subset ablation result, not the full-benchmark main result.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Section 3.4.1, prompt reduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Manual inspection is reported to show the planner agent often reducing prompt length by over 40%.

## Source Excerpt

“Manual inspection further reveals that the planner agent frequently reduces prompt length by over 40% without omitting key reasoning chains.”

## Note

This is based on manual inspection rather than a tabled benchmark protocol, so the claim is less standardized than the benchmark metrics.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Section 3.5, repeated runs with notes
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In the repeated-run memory study, average token cost is reported to drop from 104k to 93k when notes are reused.

## Source Excerpt

“Run 1 (from scratch) 64 104k 53.0” and “Run 2 (using notes) 61 (-3) 93k (-11k) 54.4 (+1.4)”.

## Note

This result comes from a repeated-run experiment on 151 instances where notes were produced, not from the full public benchmark.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Section 3.5, repeated runs with notes
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In the repeated-run memory study, Resolve Rate is reported to increase from 53.0% to 54.4% when notes from the first run are provided in the second run.

## Source Excerpt

“Run 1 (from scratch) ... 53.0” and “Run 2 (using notes) ... 54.4 (+1.4)”.

## Note

This is a repeated-run result on the subset of 151 tasks with generated notes, so it should not be read as a full-benchmark headline score.
---

The SDK uses hierarchical working memory with adaptive context compression for large-repository agent runs.

## Source Excerpt

“The Confucius SDK addresses this by providing an explicit agent context management layer that combines hierarchical working memory with adaptive context compression.”

## Note

This evidence describes the specific context-management mechanism.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.2, note-taking agent
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The note-taking agent stores persistent notes as Markdown files in a file-system-like tree.

## Source Excerpt

“Persistent notes are stored as Markdown files in a file-system-like tree: each session has an associated directory, under which the note-taking agent can create paths such as project/architecture.md, research/findings.md, or solutions/bug_fix.md.”

## Note

This is an operational detail about the note storage format and organization.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.2, hindsight notes
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The note-taking layer is described as recording failure cases alongside successful solutions.

## Source Excerpt

“A distinctive aspect of the Confucius SDK’s note-taking is its emphasis on hindsight notes for failures. The note-taking layer encourages agents to record not only successful solutions but also compilation errors, runtime exceptions, and unproductive strategies, together with eventual resolutions or reasons for abandonment.”

## Note

This evidence ties long-term memory to explicit preservation of failures, not just successes.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: Wang et al., Section 2.3.3, extensions
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The SDK factors most agent behaviors into extensions that register typed callbacks on the orchestrator loop.

## Source Excerpt

“Concretely, an extension in the Confucius SDK is a typed configuration object that registers callbacks (e.g., on_input_messages, on_plain_text, on_tag, on_llm_output). At each step of the orchestrator’s while-loop, these callbacks are invoked in a fixed order.”

## Note

This is a direct implementation detail for how capabilities are attached to the core loop.
```

```markdown
---

On a 100-example SWE-Bench-Pro subset with Claude 4 Sonnet, advanced context management is reported to improve Resolve@1 from 42.0 to 48.6.

“For Claude 4 Sonnet, advanced context management improves Resolve@1 from 42.0 to 48.6 on this subset (a +6.6 performance gain).”

This is a subset ablation result, not the full-benchmark main result.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---

evidence_id: evidence-14

---
Manual inspection is reported to show the planner agent often reducing prompt length by over 40%.

```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Section 3.5, repeated runs with notes
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In the repeated-run memory study, average token cost is reported to drop from 104k to 93k when notes are reused.

## Source Excerpt

“Run 1 (from scratch) 64 104k 53.0” and “Run 2 (using notes) 61 (-3) 93k (-11k) 54.4 (+1.4)”.

## Note

This result comes from a repeated-run experiment on 151 instances where notes were produced, not from the full public benchmark.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-032
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Wang et al., Section 3.5, repeated runs with notes
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10398
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In the repeated-run memory study, Resolve Rate is reported to increase from 53.0% to 54.4% when notes from the first run are provided in the second run.

## Source Excerpt

“Run 1 (from scratch) ... 53.0” and “Run 2 (using notes) ... 54.4 (+1.4)”.

## Note

This is a repeated-run result on the subset of 151 tasks with generated notes, so it should not be read as a full-benchmark headline score.
